selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":47,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"plumber","city_of_living":"Washington DC","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["cooking","board games","volunteering"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":98,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":42,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":66,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":11,"level":"low"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":47,"gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"plumber","city_of_living":"Washington DC","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"vocational diploma","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"no formal education"},"current_marital_status":"married","children":2,"migration_status":"native-born American","psychological_feature":"with good mental health","religious_beliefs":"Christianity","time_onsocialmedia":"You spend several hours daily on social media","hobbies":["cooking","board games","volunteering"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":98,"level":"high"},"extraversion":{"score":42,"level":"moderate"},"agreeableness":{"score":66,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":11,"level":"low"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a plumber who's spent years fixing things that don't quite work right, I see fake news on social media like a leak in the pipes. It starts small, but if you ignore it, soon you've got a flood of misinformation to deal with. Living in Washington DC, where politics and information flow like the Potomac, I know how important it is to have reliable sources. My time on social media is spent connecting with people who share my hobbies of cooking, board games, and volunteering, but even there, fake content can creep in, threatening those communities.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Given my high conscientiousness score, I believe in being meticulous about the information I consume. My moderate openness means I consider different perspectives but rely on trusted sources like the Washington Post or local DC news outlets to verify claims. As someone with good mental health and low neuroticism, I don't let fake content stress me out; instead, I address it by fact-checking and encouraging others to do the same. My background in vocational education has taught me practical skills that translate to online life: you can't fix a leak without identifying the source.",
  "tone": "Practical, patient, and community-focused",
  "citations": ["The Washington Post", "FactCheck.org"],
  "safety_notes": "none",
  "used_persona_fields": [
    "age",
    "occupation",
    "city_of_living",
    "time_onsocialmedia",
    "hobbies",
    "ocean"
  ]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a plumber who's spent years fixing things that don't quite work right, I see fake news on social media like a leak in the pipes. It starts small, but if you ignore it, soon you've got a flood of misinformation to deal with. Living in Washington DC, where politics and information flow like the Potomac, I know how important it is to have reliable sources. My time on social media is spent connecting with people who share my hobbies of cooking, board games, and volunteering, but even there, fake content can creep in, threatening those communities.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Given my high conscientiousness score, I believe in being meticulous about the information I consume. My moderate openness means I consider different perspectives but rely on trusted sources like the Washington Post or local DC news outlets to verify claims. As someone with good mental health and low neuroticism, I don't let fake content stress me out; instead, I address it by fact-checking and encouraging others to do the same. My background in vocational education has taught me practical skills that translate to online life: you can't fix a leak without identifying the source.
